items.note: nas dvoje = the two of us (a man and a woman).

items.hr: Više od sto tisuća ljudi posjetilo je festival.
items.en: More than a hundred thousand people visited the festival.
items.note: After a number phrase the verb goes singular neuter: posjetilo je.

activities.title: Vježba: brojenje s genitivom
activities.intro: Practise building the genitive plural once a count reaches five, plus the collective numerals.

questions.prompt: U dvorani je bilo osam ___.
questions.explanation: osam takes the genitive plural; stol has the long plural stolovi, so the genitive is stolova. stola would follow 2-4.

questions.prompt: Imaju ___ djece: sina i dvije kćeri.
questions.explanation: A mixed group of children takes the collective numeral troje + genitive. trojica is only for three men.

questions.prompt: Put the noun in the correct form after the number: U razredu je dvadeset pet ___. (učenik)
questions.explanation: Numbers ending in pet take the genitive plural: učenika.

questions.prompt: A genitive you met long ago, time expressions: Svakog ___ idemo na planinarenje. (vikend)
questions.explanation: Repeated time uses the temporal genitive: svakog vikenda, just like svakog jutra.

questions.prompt: Build the Croatian for: 'The two of us have five tickets for the concert.'
questions.explanation: Nas dvoje imamo pet ulaznica za koncert. pet + genitive plural ulaznica; nas dvoje takes a plural verb when it is the subject.

activities.title: Priprema proslave
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You plan a party together; every quantity forces a genitive plural or a collective numeral.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Koliko gostiju dolazi u subotu?
lines.en: How many guests are coming on Saturday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Petnaestak ljudi, među njima i troje djece.
lines.en: About fifteen people, including three children.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Trebamo li još stolica?
lines.en: Do we need more chairs?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Da, imamo samo osam, a dolazi petnaest osoba.
lines.en: Yes, we only have eight, and fifteen people are coming.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Kupit ću deset boca soka i pet kilograma mesa.
lines.en: I'll buy ten bottles of juice and five kilos of meat.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Odlično. Nas dvoje ćemo sve stići pripremiti.
lines.en: Excellent. The two of us will manage to prepare everything.

days.title: Kaže se i izgrađen je
days.objective: Choose between the se-passive for general statements and the passive participle for events and results.
days.paretoFocus: News, signs and guidebooks are written almost entirely in these two passives; controlling both lets you sound objective instead of always saying 'people do X'.
drills: Write 4 general statements about your country with the se-passive (how people eat, live, greet).
Write 4 result sentences with a passive participle (built, written, opened, closed).
Turn two active sentences into each passive type, aloud.

items: Recall: se-passive = general habit; participle = specific event or result.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Say 'Croatian is spoken here' and 'The bridge was built a hundred years ago'.

activities.title: Se-pasiv i trpni pridjev
activities.intro: The se-passive states what is generally or habitually done, with no doer in sight: Ovdje se govori hrvatski. The passive participle (trpni pridjev: izgrađen, napisan, otvoren) reports a specific event or its result, with biti: Most je izgrađen 1930. Use se for customs and rules, the participle for history and outcomes.

items.hr: U Dalmaciji se večera kasno.
items.en: In Dalmatia, dinner is eaten late.
items.note: A custom: se-passive, no agent.

items.hr: Ova se riječ piše s kvačicom.
items.en: This word is written with a diacritic hook.
items.note: A spelling rule: se-passive again.

items.hr: Most je izgrađen prije sto godina.
items.en: The bridge was built a hundred years ago.
items.note: A dated event: trpni pridjev izgrađen + je.

items.hr: Odluka je donesena jučer, a ugovor će biti potpisan sutra.
items.en: The decision was made yesterday, and the contract will be signed tomorrow.
items.note: donijeti -> donesen; the future passive uses biti + participle.

items.hr: Muzej je otvoren svaki dan osim ponedjeljka.
items.en: The museum is open every day except Monday.
items.note: otvoren works as a resultative state; osim + genitive.

activities.title: Vježba: dva pasiva
activities.intro: Decide which passive fits: a general rule with se, or an event with a participle.

questions.prompt: State the building rule naturally: U ovoj zgradi ___ ne puši.
questions.explanation: A general rule with no doer takes the se-passive: ne puši se.

questions.prompt: Katedrala ___ obnovljena nakon potresa.
questions.explanation: A specific completed event takes biti + trpni pridjev: je obnovljena.

questions.prompt: Complete with the passive participle: Roman je ___ prije dvadeset godina. (napisati)
questions.explanation: napisati forms the participle napisan; masculine to agree with roman.

questions.prompt: Blend in the relative pronoun you now know: Zgrada u ___ radim izgrađena je šezdesetih.
questions.explanation: u + locative, feminine (zgrada): u kojoj. The main clause then uses the trpni pridjev izgrađena.

questions.prompt: Build the Croatian for: 'Croatian is spoken here.'
questions.explanation: Ovdje se govori hrvatski. The se-passive with se in second position.

activities.title: Stara gradska jezgra
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You show a visitor around the old town, mixing se-passives for local customs with participles for history.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ova je jezgra prekrasna. Kad je izgrađena?
lines.en: This old centre is beautiful. When was it built?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Najstariji dio podignut je još u srednjem vijeku.
lines.en: The oldest part was put up back in the Middle Ages.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A kako se ovdje živi danas?
lines.en: And what is life like here today?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Mirno. Ljeti se dugo sjedi vani, a zimi se sve rano zatvara.
lines.en: Peaceful. In summer people sit outside late, and in winter everything closes early.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Je li kula otvorena za posjetitelje?
lines.en: Is the tower open to visitors?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Jest, obnovljena je lani i ulaz se ne naplaćuje.
lines.en: It is, it was restored last year and entry is free of charge.

days.title: Tko to radi? Pisac, čitatelj, igrač
days.objective: Recognize agent nouns and their suffixes: -telj, -ač, -ar, -ac and feminine -ica.
days.paretoFocus: Once you see that voditelj is just voditi + telj, hundreds of unknown words in any Croatian text unlock themselves for free.
drills: Derive the doer from 6 verbs you know (voditi, čitati, slušati, igrati, pjevati, kupiti).
Scan any Croatian text you have and underline three agent nouns; name their verbs.
Give the feminine pair of four professions aloud (učitelj, prevoditelj, voditelj, profesor).

items: Recall the five doer-endings: -ač, -ar, -ac, -telj and feminine -ica.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
From sluša+telj, name the word and its meaning.

activities.title: Vršitelj radnje
activities.intro: Croatian builds 'the person who does X' straight from the verb: -telj (voditi -> voditelj), -ač (igrati -> igrač), -ac (pisati -> pisac, kupiti -> kupac), and -ar for trades (zidar). Most get a feminine in -ica: učiteljica, prevoditeljica. This is a reading skill first: learn to SEE the verb inside the noun.

items.hr: Voditelj emisije predstavio je večerašnjeg gosta.
items.en: The host of the show introduced tonight's guest.
items.note: voditi -> voditelj, the one who leads.

items.hr: Čitatelji ovih novina vole komentirati vijesti.
items.en: Readers of this newspaper like to comment on the news.
items.note: čitati -> čitatelj; novine is always plural.

items.hr: Naš najbolji igrač ozlijedio je koljeno na treningu.
items.en: Our best player injured his knee at training.
items.note: igrati -> igrač, your verb family from earlier.

items.hr: Pisac je potpisivao knjige, a kupci su strpljivo čekali.
items.en: The writer was signing books, and the buyers waited patiently.
items.note: pisati -> pisac; kupiti -> kupac, with the fleeting a: kupca.

items.hr: Učiteljica i prevoditeljica sjedile su za istim stolom.
items.en: The teacher and the translator sat at the same table.
items.note: The feminine suffix -ica attaches to the -telj form.

activities.title: Vježba: od glagola do osobe
activities.intro: Practise reading and building agent nouns from verbs you already know.

questions.prompt: Which noun means 'the person who translates'?
questions.explanation: -telj marks the doer: prevoditelj. prijevod is the product and prevođenje the activity.

questions.prompt: Osoba koja sluša radio je ___.
questions.explanation: slušati + telj = slušatelj, the listener. slušanje is the act, sluh the sense of hearing.

questions.prompt: Build the agent noun: Osoba koja pjeva u zboru je ___.
questions.explanation: pjevati takes -ač: pjevač (feminine pjevačica).

questions.prompt: Comparative revision from your A2 phase: Moj je brat ___ od mene dvije godine. (star)
questions.explanation: star -> stariji, comparison with od + genitive, as you learned with adjective comparison.

questions.prompt: Build the Croatian for: 'The singer thanked the listeners.'
questions.explanation: Pjevačica je zahvalila slušateljima. zahvaliti takes the dative: slušateljima.

activities.title: Čime se bave?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You talk about family professions; every job word is an agent noun whose verb you both notice.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Čime se bavi tvoja sestra?
lines.en: What does your sister do?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ona je prevoditeljica, prevodi s njemačkoga.
lines.en: She's a translator, she translates from German.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A brat? On je bio odličan igrač, zar ne?
lines.en: And your brother? He was an excellent player, wasn't he?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Jest, ali sada radi kao trener mladih igrača.
lines.en: He was, but now he works as a coach of young players.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Zanimljivo. Ja sam oduvijek željela biti spisateljica.
lines.en: Interesting. I've always wanted to be a writer.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Pa počni pisati! Svaki pisac negdje počinje.
lines.en: Then start writing! Every writer starts somewhere.

days.title: Kućica i kućerina
days.objective: Recognize diminutives (-ić, -čić, -ica, -ce) and augmentatives (-ina, -etina, -urina) and the warmth or criticism they carry.
days.paretoFocus: Croatians rarely offer you kava; they offer kavica. Hearing the emotional colour in word endings is what makes spoken Croatian feel human instead of literal.
drills: Find the base noun inside 6 diminutives (kućica, nožić, gradić, knjižica, psić, kruščić).
Say which feeling fits: kavica, kućetina, knjižurina (warmth, envy, mockery?).
Listen for one diminutive in any Croatian audio or song today and note it.

items: Recall: -ić/-ica/-ce shrink with warmth; -etina/-urina enlarge with a critical tone.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Explain why a waiter says kavica instead of kava.

activities.title: Umanjenice i uvećanice
activities.intro: Diminutives add -ić or -čić to masculines (nož -> nožić), -ica to feminines (kuća -> kućica) and -ce to neuters (jezero -> jezerce); they often signal affection rather than size. Augmentatives add -ina, -etina or -urina (kućetina, knjižurina) and usually carry a mocking or critical tone. You need to RECOGNIZE these, not produce them yet.

items.hr: Iznajmili smo malu kućicu uz more.
items.en: We rented a little house by the sea.
items.note: kuća -> kućica: small and charming.

items.hr: Popij još jednu kavicu prije puta.
items.en: Have another little coffee before the trip.
items.note: The coffee is normal size; kavica signals warmth, not smallness.

items.hr: Dječačić je držao psića na uzici.
items.en: The little boy held a puppy on a leash.
items.note: dječak -> dječačić; pas -> psić, with the fleeting a dropping.

items.hr: Nosio je pod rukom neku knjižurinu od tisuću stranica.
items.en: He carried some huge tome of a thousand pages under his arm.
items.note: knjiga -> knjižurina: big, heavy, slightly absurd.

items.hr: Kupili su neku kućetinu na brijegu, preveliku za njih dvoje.
items.en: They bought some huge house on the hill, too big for the two of them.
items.note: kućetina: the -etina ending adds a disapproving tone.

activities.title: Vježba: veliko i malo
activities.intro: Practise recognizing what the suffix does to the noun and to the tone.

questions.prompt: What does 'gradić' mean?
questions.explanation: grad + ić = a small (often charming) town. The doer of building would be graditelj.

questions.prompt: Which word carries a critical 'huge and clumsy' tone?
questions.explanation: -urina is augmentative and mocking. knjižica is a small booklet, književnik is a writer.

questions.prompt: Complete with the affectionate form a waiter would use, in the accusative: Jeste li za jednu ___? (from kava)
questions.explanation: kava -> kavica, and after 'za jednu' it stands in the accusative: kavicu.

questions.prompt: Dative-experiencer revision: Hladno ___ je, zatvori prozor. (to me)
questions.explanation: States like cold and warm take the dative experiencer: hladno mi je, exactly like drago mi je.

questions.prompt: Build the Croatian for: 'The little girl was drawing a little house.'
questions.explanation: Djevojčica je crtala kućicu. Two diminutives: djevojčica from djevojka, kućica from kuća.

activities.title: Kod bake na kavici
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A visit to grandma overflows with diminutives, plus one giant knjižurina; notice the warmth the endings add.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dođi, sjedni! Jesi li za kavicu?
lines.en: Come, sit down! Would you like a little coffee?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Može, bako. I komadić onog kolača, molim.
lines.en: Sure, grandma. And a little piece of that cake, please.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Evo ti i kruščić, jutros je pečen.
lines.en: Here's a little bread roll too, baked this morning.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Hvala. Kakva je to knjižurina na polici?
lines.en: Thanks. What's that huge tome on the shelf?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Stari rječnik tvoga djeda. Težak je kao kamen.
lines.en: Your grandfather's old dictionary. It's heavy as a stone.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Onda ću radije uzeti ovu knjižicu s receptima.
lines.en: Then I'd rather take this little recipe booklet.

days.title: Ispričat ću ti cijelu priču
days.objective: Narrate with the pričati family: imperfective pričati for background, perfective ispričati for the telling, prepričati for retelling.
days.paretoFocus: Storytelling is the core B1 speaking skill; the pričati triplet is the engine of every anecdote, film summary and piece of gossip in Croatian.
drills: Retell yesterday in four sentences: background with pričati-type verbs, events with perfectives.
Summarize a film you like in three sentences ending with 'Prepričao/Prepričala sam ti radnju.'
Write one sentence each with pričati, ispričati and prepričati.

items: Recall: pričati = background flow, ispričati = one whole telling, prepričati = retell.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Say 'Grandma told us an interesting story' with the clitics in order.

activities.title: Pričati, ispričati, prepričati
activities.intro: In narration, imperfectives paint the background (pričali smo satima) while perfectives advance the plot (ispričala nam je priču). The prefix pre- adds 'again, onwards': prepričati is to retell or summarize someone else's story. Watch the three verbs share one root across a story.

items.hr: Dok smo putovali, baka nam je pričala o svom djetinjstvu.
items.en: While we were travelling, grandma was telling us about her childhood.
items.note: Background action: imperfective pričati.

items.hr: Jednom nam je ispričala priču koju nikad nisam zaboravio.
items.en: Once she told us a story I have never forgotten.
items.note: One complete telling: perfective ispričati.

items.hr: Sutradan sam je prepričao prijateljima u školi.
items.en: The next day I retold it to my friends at school.
items.note: prepričati = pass the story on; je here is 'it/her' in the accusative.

items.hr: Pričali smo satima i nismo primijetili da je pao mrak.
items.en: We talked for hours and didn't notice that night had fallen.
items.note: Duration takes the imperfective; pasti mrak = night falls.

items.hr: Ukratko mi prepričaj film, nemam vremena gledati ga.
items.en: Give me a short summary of the film, I don't have time to watch it.
items.note: Imperative prepričaj; nemam vremena + infinitive.

activities.title: Vježba: pripovijedanje
activities.intro: Choose the member of the pričati family that the story needs.

questions.prompt: Choose the verb that moves the story forward: Sjedili smo uz vatru i tada nam je djed ___ nešto nevjerojatno.
questions.explanation: The scene is set with imperfectives; the new complete event needs the perfective ispričao.

questions.prompt: You summarize a book for a friend: Molim te, ___ mi roman u pet rečenica.
questions.explanation: Retelling someone else's content is prepričati; ispričati is telling your own story in full.

questions.prompt: Background action in the perfekt, 1st person plural: Cijelu smo večer ___ o starim vremenima. (the imperfective of this family)
questions.explanation: An evening-long activity takes the imperfective: pričali smo.

questions.prompt: Arrival verbs from your motion lessons: Vlak je ___ na kolodvor točno u podne. (doći)
questions.explanation: doći -> došao, the perfective arrival; stigao from stići also works. kolodvor is the Croatian word for station.

questions.prompt: Build the Croatian for: 'Grandma told us an interesting story.'
questions.explanation: Baka nam je ispričala zanimljivu priču. The dative clitic nam comes before je, and the whole chain sits in second position.

activities.title: Prepričaj mi film
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend wants to hear about a film; you tell the story using the pričati verbs and guard the ending.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Kakav je bio film? Pričaj!
lines.en: How was the film? Tell me!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Odličan. Da ti ukratko prepričam radnju?
lines.en: Excellent. Shall I give you a quick summary of the plot?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Može, ali nemoj otkriti kraj.
lines.en: Sure, but don't reveal the ending.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Priča prati ženu koja se nakon dugo godina vraća u rodni grad.
lines.en: The story follows a woman who returns to her home town after many years.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Zvuči poznato. Tko ti je preporučio taj film?
lines.en: Sounds familiar. Who recommended that film to you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Kolega. Ispričao mi je pola radnje i morao sam ga pogledati.
lines.en: A colleague. He told me half the plot and I had to watch it.

days.title: Kad bih došao, on bi rekao
days.objective: Recognize the conditional used for repeated past habits (iterativni kondicional) and tell it apart from a real condition.
days.paretoFocus: Croatian memoirs, grandparents and songs all narrate the past with bi-forms; if you read them as conditions, whole childhood stories stop making sense.
drills: Read the LEARN sentences aloud and mark each bi-form as HABIT, not condition.
Describe two of your own childhood habits with 'Kad bih..., ... bi...' patterns.
Find the signal words (kad god, uvijek, svake godine) in each example.

items: Recall: bi-forms + uvijek/kad god about the past = habit, not condition.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Translate mentally: 'In the evenings we would sing together.'

activities.title: Iterativni kondicional
activities.intro: The same bih/bi/bismo forms you know from conditions also narrate REPEATED PAST HABITS: Kad bih se vratio, baka bi me čekala = whenever I came back, grandma would be waiting. Context words like uvijek, kad god, svake godine tell you it is a habit. This is a reading and listening skill; in your own speech the perfekt is always safe.

items.hr: Kad bih se vratio iz škole, baka bi me uvijek čekala s ručkom.
items.en: Whenever I came back from school, grandma would always be waiting with lunch.
items.note: Not a condition: uvijek marks a repeated habit.

items.hr: Ljeti bismo svaki dan išli na plažu.
items.en: In the summer we would go to the beach every day.
items.note: bismo + imperfective for the repeated activity.

items.hr: Djed bi svake večeri čitao novine na balkonu.
items.en: Grandfather would read the newspaper on the balcony every evening.
items.note: svake večeri: the signal of habit.

items.hr: Kad god bi padala kiša, igrali bismo se u podrumu.
items.en: Whenever it rained, we would play in the basement.
items.note: kad god = whenever; note igrati se from your verb families.

items.hr: Ponekad bi nam mama ispričala priču prije spavanja.
items.en: Sometimes mum would tell us a story before bed.
items.note: Even a perfective (ispričala) fits: each telling was complete.

activities.title: Vježba: navika ili uvjet?
activities.intro: Decide what the bi-form is doing: past habit or condition.

questions.prompt: What does 'Zimi bismo klizali na jezeru' describe?
questions.explanation: Zimi + bi-form about the past = a habit: in winter we would skate. No if-clause, no request.

questions.prompt: Recognize the habitual version: Svakog ljeta ___ kod bake na selu.
questions.explanation: svakog ljeta + conditional = the habitual past 'we would stay'. The other options are future or a question.

questions.prompt: Complete the habitual auxiliary, 1st person plural: Ljeti ___ svako jutro plivali do bove.
questions.explanation: The habitual past borrows the conditional auxiliary: bismo plivali. Standard Croatian keeps the full paradigm bih, bi, bismo, biste.

questions.prompt: Contrast with a REAL first conditional you learned earlier: Ako sutra ne bude kiše, ___ ćemo na izlet. (the verb 'to go')
questions.explanation: A real future condition uses ako + futur drugi in the if-clause and the ordinary futur in the main clause: ići ćemo.

questions.prompt: Build the Croatian for: 'In the evenings we would sing together.'
questions.explanation: Navečer bismo zajedno pjevali. The auxiliary bismo takes second position, like every enclitic.

activities.title: Djetinjstvo na otoku
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You describe childhood summers on an island; nearly every verb is a habitual bi-form.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Kako je izgledalo tvoje djetinjstvo na otoku?
lines.en: What was your childhood on the island like?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ujutro bismo pomagali djedu u polju.
lines.en: In the mornings we would help grandfather in the field.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A poslijepodne?
lines.en: And in the afternoon?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Kad bi sunce oslabjelo, trčali bismo na plažu i kupali se do mraka.
lines.en: When the sun weakened, we would run to the beach and swim until dark.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Zvuči kao raj. Nedostaje li ti to?
lines.en: Sounds like paradise. Do you miss it?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nedostaje. Svake bih večeri zaspao slušajući more.
lines.en: I do. Every evening I would fall asleep listening to the sea.

days.title: Poštovani gospodine Horvat
days.objective: Open and close formal letters and phone calls with the correct vocative: gospodine, gospođo, profesore, plus the standard formulas.
days.paretoFocus: Every job application, official email and complaint you will ever write in Croatia starts with this vocative line; get it wrong and the reader stops at word two.
drills: Write formal openings to: a Mr Novak, a Mrs Marić, a professor, a doctor (f.).
Write one complete four-line formal email: opening, request, thanks, closing.
Say the difference between 'S poštovanjem' and 'Srdačan pozdrav' aloud.

items: Recall: the title declines (gospodine), the surname stays unchanged.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Recite a full formal opening and closing from memory.

activities.title: Vokativ u službenom obraćanju
activities.intro: You learned the vocative for names and greetings long ago; formal writing has its own fixed patterns. The TITLE goes into the vocative (gospodine, gospođo, profesore, doktorice) while the SURNAME stays in the nominative: Poštovani gospodine Horvat. Close with S poštovanjem (formal) or Srdačan pozdrav (semi-formal).

items.hr: Poštovani gospodine Horvat, javljam Vam se u vezi s natječajem.
items.en: Dear Mr Horvat, I am writing to you regarding the job posting.
items.note: gospodin -> gospodine; the surname does not change.

items.hr: Poštovana gospođo Marić, zahvaljujem Vam na brzom odgovoru.
items.en: Dear Mrs Marić, thank you for your quick reply.
items.note: gospođa -> gospođo; capital Vam in formal letters.

items.hr: Poštovani profesore, molim Vas za termin konzultacija.
items.en: Dear Professor, may I ask for an office-hours appointment.
items.note: Titles decline normally: profesor -> profesore.

items.hr: Cijenjena doktorice Kovač, šaljem Vam nalaze u privitku.
items.en: Dear Dr Kovač (f.), I am sending you the results in the attachment.
items.note: doktorica -> doktorice; privitak = attachment.

items.hr: S poštovanjem, Ivana Babić
items.en: Yours faithfully, Ivana Babić
items.note: The standard formal closing; Srdačan pozdrav is a notch warmer.

activities.title: Vježba: službeno pismo
activities.intro: Practise the vocative openings and the fixed formulas of formal letters.

questions.prompt: Open a formal email to Mr Novak: ___ , javljam Vam se zbog računa.
questions.explanation: The title takes the vocative (gospodine) and the surname stays in the nominative. Dragi is for friends.

questions.prompt: Choose the closing for a job application:
questions.explanation: S poštovanjem is the neutral formal closing; the others are spoken and informal.

questions.prompt: Address a female professor at the start of a letter: Poštovana ___ , hvala Vam na pomoći. (profesorica)
questions.explanation: Feminine nouns in -ica form the vocative in -ice: profesorice.

questions.prompt: Instrumental case check from your A2 declension work: Javljam se u vezi s ___. (natječaj)
questions.explanation: s + instrumental: natječajem. Soft-stem masculines take -em.

questions.prompt: Build the Croatian for: 'Dear Madam, I am sending you the contract.'
questions.explanation: Poštovana gospođo, šaljem Vam ugovor. Vocative gospođo opens the letter; the formal Vam is capitalized.

activities.title: Poziv u ured
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You phone an office about your application; both sides keep the formal register, with vocatives for title plus surname.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dobar dan, izvolite?
lines.en: Good afternoon, how can I help you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Dobar dan, gospođo. Zovem u vezi s natječajem za posao.
lines.en: Good afternoon, madam. I'm calling about the job posting.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Kako se prezivate, gospodine?
lines.en: What is your surname, sir?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Kovač. Prijavu sam poslao prošli tjedan.
lines.en: Kovač. I sent my application last week.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Trenutak... Da, gospodine Kovač, prijava je zaprimljena.
lines.en: One moment... Yes, Mr Kovač, your application has been received.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Hvala Vam lijepa na informaciji. Doviđenja!
lines.en: Thank you very much for the information. Goodbye!

days.title: Red riječi: li, mi, ga, se, je
days.objective: Order enclitic chains correctly: li, then auxiliaries, then dative, then accusative, then se, with je last, right after conjunctions like da and kad.
days.paretoFocus: Clitic order is the single most reliable fluency signal in Croatian; 'da će mi ga vratiti' said smoothly marks you as someone who really speaks the language.
drills: Write out the chain rule from memory: li, aux, dative, accusative, se, je.
Build 4 sentences with two clitics each (mi ga, joj ih, mu se, nam je).
Say three da-clauses with clitic chains directly after da, aloud and fast.

items: Recall: je goes last and usually drops next to se.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Say 'I know that he showed it to her yesterday' with the full chain.

activities.title: Lanac enklitika
activities.intro: Croatian enclitics cluster in second position in a fixed order: (1) li, (2) verbal auxiliaries except je (sam, ćeš, bih...), (3) dative pronoun, (4) accusative or genitive pronoun, (5) se, (6) je always last. After conjunctions like da, jer and kad, the chain starts immediately.

items.hr: Rekao je da će mi ga sutra vratiti.
items.en: He said he would give it back to me tomorrow.
items.note: After da: će (aux) + mi (dative) + ga (accusative).

items.hr: Jesi li mu se javio?
items.en: Did you get in touch with him?
items.note: Question with li, then dative mu, then se.

items.hr: Ana mi se jučer napokon javila.
items.en: Ana finally got in touch with me yesterday.
items.note: Dative mi before se; the aux je is dropped next to se.

items.hr: Kad sam joj ih pokazao, nasmijala se.
items.en: When I showed them to her, she laughed.
items.note: sam (aux) + joj (dative) + ih (accusative), right after kad.

items.hr: Mislim da mu se ideja svidjela.
items.en: I think he liked the idea.
items.note: mu + se; with svidjeti se the je again disappears after se.

activities.title: Vježba: poredaj enklitike
activities.intro: Practise the fixed chain, especially right after conjunctions.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct order: Obećao je ___ vratiti do petka.
questions.explanation: After da: auxiliary će first, then dative mi, then accusative ga.

questions.prompt: Which sentence has the correct clitic order?
questions.explanation: Dative mi precedes se, and the chain sits in second position after the first stressed word.

questions.prompt: Insert the missing auxiliary in its slot: Kad ___ joj ih dala, zahvalila mi je. (biti, 1st person singular)
questions.explanation: The chain after kad runs aux + dative + accusative: sam joj ih.

questions.prompt: Question forms from your first weeks, now with a chain: ___ li je vidio? (asking 'did you see her')
questions.explanation: Yes-no questions use the stressed auxiliary + li: Jesi li. The je here is the accusative 'her', not the auxiliary.

questions.prompt: Build the Croatian for: 'I know that he showed it to her yesterday.'
questions.explanation: Znam da joj ga je jučer pokazao. Dative joj, accusative ga, and je last: the full textbook chain.

activities.title: Posuđena knjiga
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A conversation about a borrowed book where every reply carries a clitic chain; keep each one in order.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Jesi li mu napokon vratila knjigu?
lines.en: Did you finally return the book to him?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nisam još. Obećala sam da ću mu je donijeti sutra.
lines.en: Not yet. I promised I'd bring it to him tomorrow.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Rekao mi je da mu se žuri.
lines.en: He told me he's in a hurry.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Znam, znam. Javit ću mu se večeras.
lines.en: I know, I know. I'll get in touch with him tonight.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ako je zaboraviš, naljutit će se.
lines.en: If you forget it, he'll get angry.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Neću je zaboraviti, već sam je stavila u torbu.
lines.en: I won't forget it, I've already put it in my bag.

days.title: U putničkoj agenciji: rezervacija i reklamacija
days.objective: Book a trip, complain when it goes wrong, and ask for a refund with polite conditionals and passive participles.
days.paretoFocus: Trips fail on schedule; the language of reklamacija and povrat novca is where politeness, passives and case endings all earn their keep at once.
drills: Write your dream booking request in two polite sentences with 'Želio/Željela bih...'.
Write a three-sentence complaint: what was promised, what happened, what you want.
List 6 trip words (let, smještaj, povrat, reklamacija, otkazan, uključen) with translations.

items: Recall: povrat novca = refund; povratak = a person's return.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Say 'I would like to cancel my reservation' politely.

activities.title: Rezervacija, otkazivanje, povrat
activities.intro: Trip language runs on the polite conditional (želio bih rezervirati), passive participles for what went wrong (let je otkazan, prijevoz je uključen) and a few fixed nouns: reklamacija (complaint), povrat novca (refund), smještaj (accommodation).

items.hr: Želio bih rezervirati putovanje za dvije osobe.
items.en: I would like to book a trip for two people.
items.note: The conditional softens the request; feminine speakers say željela bih.

items.hr: Je li u cijenu uključen doručak?
items.en: Is breakfast included in the price?
items.note: Question with je li; uključen is a passive participle.

items.hr: Let nam je otkazan, a nitko nas nije obavijestio.
items.en: Our flight was cancelled, and nobody informed us.
items.note: otkazati -> otkazan; note the chain nam je.

items.hr: Želim uložiti reklamaciju i tražim povrat novca.
items.en: I want to file a complaint and I am asking for a refund.
items.note: uložiti reklamaciju and povrat novca are fixed phrases.

items.hr: Možemo Vam ponuditi zamjenski smještaj ili povrat cijelog iznosa.
items.en: We can offer you replacement accommodation or a refund of the full amount.
items.note: The agency's side: zamjenski = replacement, iznos = amount.

activities.title: Vježba: kad putovanje krene po zlu
activities.intro: Practise complaints, refunds and the grammar inside them.

questions.prompt: Your flight was cancelled. Complain with the right clitics: Let ___ otkazan bez ikakve obavijesti.
questions.explanation: Dative nam ('on us') comes before je, which stands last in the chain: let nam je otkazan.

questions.prompt: You want your money back: Tražim ___ novca.
questions.explanation: povrat novca is the refund; povratak is a person's return (povratak kući).

questions.prompt: Ask about transfers with the participle of 'uključiti': Je li prijevoz ___ u cijenu?
questions.explanation: uključiti -> uključen, masculine to agree with prijevoz.

questions.prompt: Aspect decision from your verb pairs: Jučer sam ___ dvije karte za trajekt. (kupovati or kupiti, feminine perfekt)
questions.explanation: One completed purchase takes the perfective kupiti: kupila sam. kupovala would suggest a drawn-out process.

questions.prompt: Build the Croatian for: 'I would like to cancel my reservation.'
questions.explanation: Želio bih otkazati svoju rezervaciju. Polite conditional plus svoju, because the reservation belongs to the subject.

activities.title: Reklamacija u agenciji
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Back from a bad package trip, you file a complaint and negotiate a refund, politely but firmly.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dobar dan, kako Vam mogu pomoći?
lines.en: Good afternoon, how can I help you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Dobar dan. Vratili smo se s putovanja i želim uložiti reklamaciju.
lines.en: Good afternoon. We're back from our trip and I want to file a complaint.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Što se dogodilo?
lines.en: What happened?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Hotel nije bio onaj koji smo rezervirali, a klima nije radila.
lines.en: The hotel wasn't the one we booked, and the air conditioning didn't work.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Žao mi je zbog neugodnosti. Možemo Vam ponuditi popust za sljedeće putovanje.
lines.en: I'm sorry for the inconvenience. We can offer you a discount on your next trip.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Radije bih povrat dijela novca, ako je moguće.
lines.en: I would rather have part of the money back, if possible.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: U redu. Ispunite obrazac i novac će biti vraćen u roku od trideset dana.
lines.en: All right. Fill in the form and the money will be returned within thirty days.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Hvala, to mi odgovara.
lines.en: Thank you, that works for me.

days.title: Žao mi je, drago mi je, bojim se
days.objective: Express feelings and react to news with dative constructions (žao mi je, drago mi je, nedostaje mi) plus bojati se and ponosan na.
days.paretoFocus: Reacting warmly is the social glue of B1 conversation; these five constructions cover sympathy, joy, fear, pride and longing, the whole emotional keyboard.
drills: React to three imagined pieces of news (good, bad, scary) with the right construction.
Write two sentences about people you are proud of with ponosan/ponosna na + accusative.
Say what and whom you miss with nedostaje mi / nedostaju mi, watching the agreement.

items: Recall: the feeler is DATIVE (žao mi je); nedostajati agrees with what is missed.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Say 'I'm afraid we'll be late' and 'I'm proud of you'.

activities.title: Osjećaji i reakcije
activities.intro: Croatian feelings often put the feeler in the dative: žao mi je (I'm sorry), drago mi je (I'm glad), nedostaje mi (I miss). bojati se takes the genitive or a da-clause; ponosan goes with na + accusative. The thing missed is the SUBJECT of nedostajati, so the verb agrees with it.

items.hr: Žao mi je što nisam mogla doći na proslavu.
items.en: I am sorry I could not come to the celebration.
items.note: žao + dative + je; što introduces the reason.

items.hr: Drago nam je da ste se sretno vratili.
items.en: We are glad you got back safely.
items.note: Any dative fits the pattern: drago nam je, drago joj je.

items.hr: Bojim se da ćemo zakasniti na vlak.
items.en: I am afraid we will miss the train.
items.note: bojati se da + futur for feared outcomes.

items.hr: Ponosna sam na svoju kćer, upisala je medicinu.
items.en: I am proud of my daughter, she got into medical school.
items.note: ponosan/ponosna na + accusative.

items.hr: Nedostaje mi obitelj otkad živim u inozemstvu.
items.en: I have missed my family since I moved abroad.
items.note: obitelj is the subject; with a plural it becomes nedostaju mi prijatelji.

activities.title: Vježba: kako reagirati
activities.intro: Pick the right emotional construction and the case it demands.

questions.prompt: React to sad news about a friend's grandmother: ___ mi je zbog tvoje bake.
questions.explanation: Sympathy is žao mi je; drago mi je would mean you are glad.

questions.prompt: Express pride with the right preposition and case: Ponosni smo ___.
questions.explanation: ponosan na + accusative: na tebe. na tebi would be locative, a location.

questions.prompt: Show you are pleased someone came: ___ mi je što si došao.
questions.explanation: drago mi je što... = I am glad that...

questions.prompt: Genitive government you met with verbs long ago: Kao dijete bojao sam se ___. (mrak)
questions.explanation: bojati se + genitive: mraka. The same genitive government you saw with the čuti-type verbs.

questions.prompt: Build the Croatian for: 'I miss my friends from school.'
questions.explanation: Nedostaju mi prijatelji iz škole. prijatelji is the subject, so the verb is plural: nedostaju.

activities.title: Prije rezultata ispita
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You confide a worry to a friend; the exchange moves through fear, sympathy, encouragement and gratitude.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Izgledaš zabrinuto. Što se dogodilo?
lines.en: You look worried. What happened?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Bojim se da nisam prošla ispit.
lines.en: I'm afraid I didn't pass the exam.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Žao mi je što se toliko brineš. Kad ćeš saznati rezultate?
lines.en: I'm sorry you're worrying so much. When will you find out the results?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sutra. Sram me je reći roditeljima.
lines.en: Tomorrow. I'm ashamed to tell my parents.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Nemoj se brinuti unaprijed. Ponosni su na tebe bez obzira na sve.
lines.en: Don't worry in advance. They're proud of you no matter what.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Hvala ti. Drago mi je što te imam.
lines.en: Thank you. I'm glad I have you.

days.title: Želite li promijeniti svijet? Volontiranje
days.objective: Argue for or against giving your free time to volunteer work, stating a position with a reason using words like udruga, pomoć and učiniti.
days.paretoFocus: Civic action is an officially required B1 topic, and it naturally carries top-frequency everyday words such as važan, učiniti and isti that every serious conversation needs.
drills: Say aloud three reasons why volunteering is or is not worth your time, each starting with 'Mislim da'.
List five things a local udruga could do for your street, in Croatian.
Record yourself inviting a friend to a park clean-up in two sentences.

items: How do you form a polite invitation with the plural imperative of doći?
Which verb form follows trebati and other modals in standard Croatian?
Give the perfective partner of činiti and use it in a sentence about helping someone.

activities.title: Udruga, akcija i pomoć
activities.intro: At B1 you defend an opinion, not just state facts. These sentences model taking a position on volunteering and backing it with jer or zato. Watch the high-frequency words važan, učiniti and pomoć doing the real work.

items.hr: Mislim da je volontiranje važno jer čovjek najviše nauči kad pomaže drugima.
items.en: I think volunteering is important because a person learns the most when helping others.
items.note: važan = important; neuter važno after je

items.hr: Postao sam član jedne udruge jer želim učiniti nešto korisno za svoj grad.
items.en: I became a member of an association because I want to do something useful for my city.
items.note: učiniti = to do (perfective), one of the most common spoken verbs

items.hr: Ne slažem se s ljudima koji kažu da jedna osoba ne može ništa promijeniti, jer svaka velika akcija počinje malom odlukom.
items.en: I disagree with people who say that one person cannot change anything, because every big action starts with a small decision.

items.hr: Sudjelovala sam u akciji čišćenja plaže jer volim odmah vidjeti rezultat svoga rada.
items.en: I took part in a beach clean-up action because I like to see the result of my work right away.
items.note: sudjelovati u + locative = to take part in

items.hr: Besplatna pomoć starijim susjedima vrijedi po mojem mišljenju više od novca jer gradi povjerenje u cijelom kvartu.
items.en: Free help for elderly neighbours is in my opinion worth more than money because it builds trust in the whole neighbourhood.
items.note: pomoć = help (feminine i-noun)

activities.title: Akcija i pomoć
activities.intro: Practise stating and defending opinions about civic action.

questions.prompt: Complete the sentence: 'Trebam ___ nešto korisno za svoju zajednicu.'
questions.explanation: Croatian modals like trebati take the infinitive. 'Trebam da učinim' follows the Serbian pattern and is not standard Croatian.

questions.prompt: Your neighbour is shy about joining the clean-up. Which polite invitation is correct?
options: Da dođete s nama u subotu!
questions.explanation: A quick return to the imperative: the polite plural imperative of doći is dođite. The other options are a past participle, a plain present and a non-standard da-construction.

questions.prompt: Ona je aktivna ___ udruge koja pomaže starijim ljudima.
questions.explanation: A woman who belongs to an organization is a članica; a man is a član. The feminine form agrees with aktivna.

questions.prompt: Volontiram svake subote jer mislim da je ___ pomagati ljudima.
questions.explanation: The adjective važan takes its neuter form važno in impersonal statements after je.

questions.prompt: Build the Croatian for: 'I want to change something in my city.'
questions.explanation: Željeti takes the infinitive promijeniti; the indefinite pronoun nešto is its object, and u + locative gives the place.

activities.title: Vrijedi li volontirati?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Your friend thinks volunteering is a waste of time; defend it with concrete reasons and concede one fair point.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Iskreno, mislim da je volontiranje gubitak vremena jer nitko ne cijeni besplatan rad.
lines.en: Honestly, I think volunteering is a waste of time because nobody values free work.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ne slažem se s tobom. Lani sam volontirao u jednoj udruzi i naučio sam više nego na plaćenom poslu.
lines.en: I don't agree with you. Last year I volunteered in an association and I learned more than at a paid job.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ali što si stvarno promijenio? Svijet je ostao isti.
lines.en: But what did you really change? The world stayed the same.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Za ljude kojima smo pomogli nije isti. Njima je naša pomoć bila jako važna.
lines.en: For the people we helped it is not the same. For them our help was very important.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dobro, ali taj posao bi netko trebao platiti, a ne tražiti besplatne ruke.
lines.en: Fine, but someone should pay for that work, not look for free hands.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Tu imaš pravo. Ipak, dok čekamo bolji sustav, netko mora nešto učiniti odmah.
lines.en: There you are right. Still, while we wait for a better system, someone has to do something right away.

days.title: Želite li promijeniti svijet? Peticija i okoliš
days.objective: Discuss petitions, protests and environmental measures and defend which form of civic pressure you find most effective.
days.paretoFocus: This completes the officially required civic-action topic and anchors the everyday verbs dogoditi se and pokazati plus the news-register nouns mjera and sredstvo.
drills: Name three problems in your neighbourhood and one measure you would propose for each, aloud in Croatian.
Write two sentences about something that happened in your city this month, using the past tense of dogoditi se.
Argue in one breath: petition or protest, which works better and why.

items: Which case follows the prepositions protiv and zbog?
When do you choose the perfective past instead of the imperfective for a finished result?
What is the standard Croatian word for the environment?

activities.title: Peticija, prosvjed i mjere
activities.intro: Opinions about public action need verbs of happening and showing. Watch dogoditi se (to happen) and pokazati (to show), plus mjera (measure) and sredstvo (means), two nouns the news register uses constantly.

items.hr: Potpisala sam peticiju za čišći zrak jer se zagađenje tiče svih nas, a ne samo onih s automobilom.
items.en: I signed a petition for cleaner air because pollution concerns all of us, not only those with a car.
items.note: ticati se + genitive = to concern

items.hr: Mislim da miran prosvjed ima smisla jer vlast često sluša tek kad građani izađu na ulicu.
items.en: I think a peaceful protest makes sense because the authorities often listen only when citizens go out into the street.
items.note: prosvjed = protest (standard Croatian)

items.hr: Grad je uveo nove mjere za odvajanje otpada i po mojem mišljenju to je dobar prvi korak jer se rezultat već vidi na ulicama.
items.en: The city introduced new measures for separating waste, and in my opinion that is a good first step because the result can already be seen in the streets.
items.note: mjera = measure

items.hr: U našem se kvartu dogodilo nešto važno: susjedi su prvi put zajedno pokazali da im nije svejedno.
items.en: Something important happened in our neighbourhood: for the first time the neighbours showed together that they do care.
items.note: dogoditi se = to happen; pokazati = to show

items.hr: Neki kažu da peticija ništa ne mijenja, ali ja smatram da je ona korisno sredstvo jer okuplja ljude oko zajedničkog cilja.
items.en: Some say a petition changes nothing, but I consider it a useful means because it gathers people around a common goal.
items.note: sredstvo = means, instrument

activities.title: Akcija za okoliš
activities.intro: Practise the language of civic pressure and environmental action.

questions.prompt: Choose the standard Croatian word: 'Moramo čuvati ___ za buduće generacije.'
questions.explanation: Okoliš is the standard Croatian word for the environment. Životna sredina is the Serbian term, vazduh is Serbian for air, and okolišem is the wrong case here.

questions.prompt: Skupljali smo potpise cijeli mjesec i na kraju smo ih ___ više od dvije tisuće.
questions.explanation: A look back at aspect: imperfective skupljali describes the month-long process, while perfective skupili reports the completed result at the end.

questions.prompt: Što se ___ na sastanku stanara dok me nije bilo?
questions.explanation: Dogoditi se means to happen; in the past tense with što the participle is neuter: dogodilo.

questions.prompt: Rezultati ankete jasno su ___ da građani žele više parkova, a manje parkirališta.
questions.explanation: Pokazati means to show; the masculine plural subject rezultati takes the participle pokazali.

questions.prompt: Build the Croatian for: 'Nothing will change if we stay silent.'
questions.explanation: Ništa is the subject, the reflexive se sits before the negated future neće, and ako introduces the condition in the present tense.

activities.title: Peticija ili prosvjed?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You believe petitions work; your friend believes only street protests do. Weigh both sides honestly and finish with your own position.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Peticije su mi smiješne. Tisuću potpisa na papiru ništa ne znači, samo prosvjed nešto mijenja.
lines.en: Petitions seem ridiculous to me. A thousand signatures on paper mean nothing, only a protest changes something.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nisam siguran. Kod nas se prošle godine dogodilo suprotno: peticija protiv zatvaranja knjižnice uspjela je bez ijednog prosvjeda.
lines.en: I'm not sure. Last year the opposite happened in our town: a petition against closing the library succeeded without a single protest.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: To je iznimka. Vlast reagira tek kad vidi pet tisuća ljudi na trgu.
lines.en: That's an exception. The authorities react only when they see five thousand people on the square.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Možda, ali prosvjed bez jasnog zahtjeva brzo se zaboravi. Papir ostaje kao sredstvo pritiska.
lines.en: Maybe, but a protest without a clear demand is quickly forgotten. The paper remains as a means of pressure.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Znači, ti bi samo tiho skupljao potpise dok se ništa ne mijenja?
lines.en: So you would just quietly collect signatures while nothing changes?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ne, tvrdim da jedno bez drugoga ne radi: potpisi pokazuju broj, a ulica pokazuje volju.
lines.en: No, I claim that one doesn't work without the other: signatures show the numbers, and the street shows the will.

days.title: Vrijednosti, sposobnosti i interesi
days.objective: Talk about what you value, what you are good at and what interests you, defending your priorities with reasons.
days.paretoFocus: This is an officially required B1 topic the course never themed, and it hosts the abstract core nouns vrijednost, sposobnost and interes plus the everyday words isti, značiti and vlastit.
drills: Name your three strongest values in Croatian and justify the first one with a jer-clause.
Describe one ability you have and one you would like to develop, in two Croatian sentences.
Explain aloud what your favourite hobby means to you, using the verb značiti.

items: Which case do nouns take after the numbers pet and higher?
How does the adjective isti change in the accusative masculine plural?
What does the dative experiencer look like in 'Važno mi je...'?

activities.title: Što mi je zaista važno
activities.intro: Values, abilities and interests are classic interview and exam territory. Each sentence takes a stand and defends it. Note vlastit (one's own), a stronger version of svoj, and the verb značiti (to mean).

items.hr: Za mene je poštenje najveća vrijednost jer bez njega nijedan odnos ne traje dugo.
items.en: For me honesty is the greatest value because without it no relationship lasts long.
items.note: vrijednost = value (feminine i-noun)

items.hr: Mislim da svatko ima neku posebnu sposobnost, samo je mora otkriti i vježbati.
items.en: I think everyone has some special ability, they just have to discover it and practise it.
items.note: sposobnost = ability

items.hr: Moji interesi i moj posao nisu isto, i to mi uopće ne smeta jer hobi čuvam samo za sebe.
items.en: My interests and my job are not the same thing, and that doesn't bother me at all because I keep my hobby just for myself.
items.note: isti = the same; here neuter isto

items.hr: Znati strani jezik danas znači imati otvorena vrata u pola Europe, zato učim hrvatski svaki dan.
items.en: Knowing a foreign language today means having open doors in half of Europe, which is why I study Croatian every day.
items.note: značiti = to mean

items.hr: Ne bih mijenjao vlastiti mir za veću plaću jer zdravlje po mojem mišljenju vrijedi više od novca.
items.en: I wouldn't trade my own peace for a bigger salary because in my opinion health is worth more than money.
items.note: vlastit = one's own

activities.title: Vrijednosti i izbori
activities.intro: Practise talking about values, abilities and interests with correct agreement.

questions.prompt: Ana i ja imamo ___ interese, pa nam nikad nije dosadno zajedno.
questions.explanation: Interese is accusative masculine plural, so the adjective isti takes the ending -e: iste interese.

questions.prompt: U prijavi za posao navela je znanje pet stranih ___.
questions.explanation: Remember the rule from the numbers lessons: after pet and higher numbers the noun stands in the genitive plural, jezika.

questions.prompt: On ima rijetku ___ da smiri ljude kad se posvađaju.
questions.explanation: Sposobnost (ability) is a feminine i-noun; its accusative singular looks the same as the nominative.

questions.prompt: Ta mi je riječ potpuno nova. Što ona ___?
questions.explanation: Značiti means to mean; the third person singular present is znači.

questions.prompt: Build the Croatian for: 'It is important to me to have my own opinion.'
questions.explanation: The impersonal Važno je takes a dative experiencer mi in second position, followed by the infinitive imati and its object vlastito mišljenje.

activities.title: Plaća ili slobodno vrijeme?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Your friend would accept a higher salary that costs every Saturday; you would not. Defend your values while granting the fair points.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ponudili su mi trideset posto veću plaću, ali radit ću i subotom. Naravno da prihvaćam, novac je novac.
lines.en: They offered me a thirty percent higher salary, but I'll work Saturdays too. Of course I'm accepting, money is money.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja ne bih prihvatio. Subota mi previše znači: obitelj, sport, mir. To se ne može kupiti.
lines.en: I wouldn't accept. Saturday means too much to me: family, sport, peace. That can't be bought.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Lako je tebi govoriti. Veća plaća znači veći stan i bolji auto.
lines.en: Easy for you to say. A bigger salary means a bigger flat and a better car.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Istina, ali kakva je korist od stana u kojem te nikad nema? Vlastito vrijeme je isto vrijednost.
lines.en: True, but what use is a flat you are never in? Your own time is a value too.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dobro, ali ja sam mlad i sada je trenutak za rad. Odmarat ću se poslije.
lines.en: Fine, but I'm young and now is the moment for work. I'll rest later.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Djelomično se slažem, samo pazi da to poslije stvarno dođe. Znam ljude koji su ga čekali trideset godina.
lines.en: I partly agree, just make sure that 'later' really comes. I know people who waited thirty years for it.

days.title: Život i smrt
days.objective: Talk about birth, death and remembrance with the right verbs and express a considered opinion on how openly these topics should be discussed.
days.paretoFocus: The deck has no life-and-death vocabulary at all; this lesson hosts umrijeti, mrtav, smrt, roditi se and naslijediti, all inside the spoken top frequency band.
drills: Say when and where you were born in one Croatian sentence using roditi se.
Retell in three sentences the life of an older relative, ending with a memory of them.
Practise the irregular participles aloud: umrijeti, umro, umrla; ten repetitions with different subjects.

items: What are the masculine and feminine past participles of umrijeti?
How does the relative pronoun koji take its case from its own clause?
Why does nikada require a negated verb in the same sentence?

activities.title: Od rođenja do groba
activities.intro: Croatian speakers use these words daily, in biography, family stories and news. The key verbs are roditi se (to be born) and umrijeti (to die), with the irregular participle umro. Each sentence also takes a small position worth arguing about.

items.hr: Rodio sam se u malom selu kraj rijeke, ali mislim da mjesto rođenja ne određuje cijeli život.
items.en: I was born in a small village by the river, but I think the place of birth does not determine one's whole life.
items.note: roditi se = to be born

items.hr: Moj djed je umro prošle zime i tek sam tada shvatio koliko su mi njegove priče značile.
items.en: My grandfather died last winter and only then did I realize how much his stories meant to me.
items.note: umrijeti -> umro; shvatiti = to realize

items.hr: Smrt je dio života, ali kod nas o njoj gotovo nitko ne želi govoriti, i to mi se čini pogrešnim.
items.en: Death is a part of life, but among us almost nobody wants to talk about it, and that seems wrong to me.
items.note: smrt = death (feminine i-noun)

items.hr: Jednom godišnje cijela obitelj posjeti bakin grob jer nam je sjećanje na nju važno.
items.en: Once a year the whole family visits grandma's grave because the memory of her is important to us.
items.note: jednom = once; grob = grave

items.hr: Naslijedila je staru kuću i odlučila je obnoviti jer kuća bez ljudi brzo postaje mrtva.
items.en: She inherited the old house and decided to renovate it because a house without people quickly becomes dead.
items.note: naslijediti = to inherit; mrtav = dead

activities.title: Život, smrt i sjećanje
activities.intro: Practise the verbs and nouns of life events, including the irregular participle of umrijeti.

questions.prompt: Njezin je stari susjed ___ prije dvije godine.
questions.explanation: The past participle of umrijeti is irregular: umro, umrla. Umreo is the ekavian form and not standard Croatian.

questions.prompt: Ovo je fotografija bake ___ sam najviše volio.
questions.explanation: Back to the relative pronoun: koji agrees with baka in gender but takes its case from its own clause. As the object of volio it is accusative feminine, koju.

questions.prompt: Naša se kći ___ u proljeće, na sam Uskrs.
questions.explanation: Roditi se is reflexive; the feminine singular participle is rodila.

questions.prompt: Vratili smo se s planine gladni, promrzli i napola ___ od umora.
questions.explanation: Mrtav (dead) is common in figurative use too; the masculine plural form is mrtvi.

questions.prompt: Build the Croatian for: 'I will never forget that day.'
questions.explanation: Nikada requires the negated future neću; Croatian stacks negatives, and taj dan is the accusative object.

activities.title: Treba li s djecom razgovarati o smrti?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Your friend wants to shield children from the topic of death; you argue for honest conversation, and you both look for a middle ground.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Mislim da djecu treba čuvati od teških tema. O smrti neka uče kad odrastu.
lines.en: I think children should be protected from hard topics. Let them learn about death when they grow up.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ne slažem se. Kad je umro naš pas, kći je imala pet godina i pitala je sve. Šutnja bi bila gora.
lines.en: I disagree. When our dog died, my daughter was five and she asked everything. Silence would have been worse.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ali dijete to ne može shvatiti. Samo ćeš ga uplašiti.
lines.en: But a child can't grasp that. You'll only scare them.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Djeca shvate više nego što mislimo. Strah raste baš iz onoga o čemu se šuti.
lines.en: Children understand more than we think. Fear grows precisely from what is kept silent.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Možda, ali postoji granica. Ja dijete ne bih vodio na pogreb.
lines.en: Maybe, but there is a limit. I wouldn't take a child to a funeral.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Tu se možemo složiti da ovisi o djetetu. Ja bih ga pitao želi li ići, jer i dijete ima pravo na oproštaj.
lines.en: There we can agree that it depends on the child. I would ask them if they want to go, because a child also has a right to say goodbye.

days.title: Tijelo i zdravlje II
days.objective: Describe pain, injuries and check-ups precisely and defend an opinion about prevention versus pushing through.
days.paretoFocus: Hosts the missing high-frequency body words tijelo, bol, ozljeda, stanje, koža and spreman, all needed for any doctor's visit or sports conversation.
drills: Point at five body parts and name a possible problem for each in Croatian.
Describe your last visit to a doctor in three sentences, using pregled and stanje.
Say aloud two sentences with boli me and bole me, one singular and one plural.

items: How do you give hypothetical advice with the conditional and na tvome mjestu?
When does the verb boljeti appear as boli and when as bole?
Which pronoun form names the person in pain with boljeti?

activities.title: Tijelo, bol i pregled
activities.intro: The body vocabulary here fills real gaps: tijelo (body), bol (pain, feminine), ozljeda (injury), stanje (condition). The pain construction is impersonal: boli me, bole me. Each sentence argues a small health position.

items.hr: Idem na pregled svake godine jer mislim da je bolje spriječiti nego liječiti.
items.en: I go for a check-up every year because I think it is better to prevent than to cure.
items.note: pregled = check-up, examination

items.hr: Nakon prvog treninga boljelo me je cijelo tijelo, ali smatram da je takva bol znak da se nešto mijenja.
items.en: After the first training session my whole body hurt, but I consider that kind of pain a sign that something is changing.
items.note: tijelo = body; bol = pain (feminine)

items.hr: Ozljeda koljena naučila me da tijelo nije stroj i da odmor nije lijenost.
items.en: A knee injury taught me that the body is not a machine and that rest is not laziness.
items.note: ozljeda = injury

items.hr: Liječnica kaže da je moje stanje dobro, ali da moram provoditi više vremena na svježem zraku.
items.en: The doctor says my condition is good, but that I have to spend more time in the fresh air.
items.note: stanje = condition, state

items.hr: Spreman sam promijeniti prehranu jer mi je koža zbog stresa postala jako suha.
items.en: I am ready to change my diet because my skin has become very dry from stress.
items.note: spreman = ready; koža = skin

activities.title: Kod liječnika i poslije
activities.intro: Practise describing pain, injuries and readiness with correct impersonal constructions.

questions.prompt: Pao je s bicikla, ali na sreću ___ nije ozbiljna.
questions.explanation: Ozljeda (injury) is the subject, so it stands in the nominative and agrees with the feminine adjective ozbiljna.

questions.prompt: Poslije treninga me ___ ruke i leđa.
questions.explanation: The things that hurt are the subject: ruke i leđa is plural, so the verb is bole. The person in pain is the accusative me.

questions.prompt: Na tvome mjestu ___ odmah liječniku.
questions.explanation: Advice often revisits the conditional: bih plus the past participle. Na tvome mjestu signals a hypothetical situation, so the plain future does not fit.

questions.prompt: Trener nas je pitao jesmo li ___ za sutrašnju utrku.
questions.explanation: Spreman (ready) agrees with the plural subject: spremni.

questions.prompt: Build the Croatian for: 'My back hurts when I sit for too long.'
questions.explanation: Leđa is a plural-only noun, so the verb is bole; the sufferer appears as accusative me right after the verb.

activities.title: Boli me, ali treniram dalje
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Your friend keeps training through shoulder pain; argue for seeing a doctor, and separate tiredness from injury.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Rame me boli već tjedan dana, ali trening ne prekidam. Bol je dio sporta.
lines.en: My shoulder has been hurting for a week, but I'm not stopping training. Pain is part of sport.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: To mi zvuči ludo. Bol je poruka tijela, a ne znak snage.
lines.en: That sounds crazy to me. Pain is the body's message, not a sign of strength.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ako staneš kod svake sitnice, nikad nećeš napredovati.
lines.en: If you stop at every little thing, you'll never make progress.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Postoji razlika između umora i ozljede. Umor prođe, a ozljeda bez odmora postane kronična.
lines.en: There is a difference between tiredness and an injury. Tiredness passes, but an injury without rest becomes chronic.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Pregled košta vremena i novca, a vjerojatno nije ništa.
lines.en: A check-up costs time and money, and it's probably nothing.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Možda nije ništa, ali ako jest, platit ćeš poslije puno više. Ja bih na tvom mjestu otišao odmah.
lines.en: Maybe it's nothing, but if it is something, you'll pay much more later. In your place I would go right away.

days.title: Grad, županija i država
days.objective: Discuss how local and state administration touches everyday life and defend an opinion about where decisions should be made.
days.paretoFocus: Hosts the civic web-register core the deck lacks: uprava, županija, ministarstvo, građanin, vlast, područje, plus the very frequent preposition prema.
drills: Name in Croatian three documents you have needed from an office and where you got them.
State one thing your county does well and one it does badly, each with a jer-clause.
Build three sentences with prema + dative: according to the law, according to the plan, according to my experience.

items: Which case follows u when you say where you live?
Which case do nouns take after dvadeset?
What does prema mean and which case does it govern?

activities.title: Građani i uprava
activities.intro: Civic vocabulary dominates Croatian news and official letters: uprava (administration), županija (county), ministarstvo (ministry), vlast (the authorities). The preposition prema with the dative means 'according to' and appears constantly in this register.

items.hr: Prema mojem iskustvu gradska uprava danas radi brže nego prije jer se mnogo toga rješava preko interneta.
items.en: According to my experience the city administration works faster today than before because a lot is handled over the internet.
items.note: prema + dative = according to; uprava = administration

items.hr: Naša županija najviše ulaže u ceste, a ja mislim da bi škole trebale biti na prvom mjestu jer djeca ne mogu čekati.
items.en: Our county invests most in roads, and I think schools should come first because children cannot wait.
items.note: županija = county

items.hr: Ministarstvo je najavilo novi zakon o zaštiti podataka i građani su odmah podijelili mišljenja.
items.en: The ministry announced a new data-protection law and citizens immediately split into opposing opinions.
items.note: ministarstvo = ministry; građanin = citizen

items.hr: Svako područje ima svoje probleme, zato smatram da se odluke ne smiju donositi samo u glavnom gradu.
items.en: Every area has its own problems, which is why I believe decisions must not be made only in the capital.
items.note: područje = area, region

items.hr: Po mojem mišljenju vlast mora objasniti svaku svoju odluku jer bez toga ljudi gube povjerenje u državu.
items.en: In my opinion the authorities must explain every decision of theirs because without that people lose trust in the state.
items.note: vlast = the authorities (feminine i-noun)

activities.title: Papiri i uprava
activities.intro: Practise the civic register: offices, counties and rules.

questions.prompt: Za novu osobnu iskaznicu moram otići u policijsku ___.
questions.explanation: Policijska uprava is the general term for a police administration office; u + accusative marks the destination, so uprava becomes upravu.

questions.prompt: ___ novom zakonu, svaki građanin ima pravo vidjeti svoje podatke.
questions.explanation: Prema with the dative means 'according to'; it is one of the most frequent prepositions in news and official language.

questions.prompt: Cijeli život živi u istoj ___.
questions.explanation: Where you live takes u plus the locative; feminine a-nouns end in -i. Notice the locative of isti as well: u istoj županiji.

questions.prompt: Hrvatska je podijeljena na dvadeset ___ i Grad Zagreb.
questions.explanation: Numbers from five upwards take the genitive plural. The genitive plural of županija happens to look like the nominative singular: dvadeset županija.

questions.prompt: Build the Croatian for: 'The city must listen to its citizens.'
questions.explanation: Morati takes the infinitive; svoje refers back to the subject grad, and građane is the accusative plural of građanin.

activities.title: Sve se odlučuje u glavnom gradu
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Your friend from a small town says the capital decides everything; you defend the need for central planning while conceding what is unfair.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Kod nas se ništa ne pita ljude iz manjih mjesta. Sve se odlučuje u glavnom gradu, i to me ljuti.
lines.en: Nobody asks people from smaller places anything. Everything is decided in the capital, and that makes me angry.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Djelomično imaš pravo, ali netko mora gledati cijelu državu, a ne samo jedno područje.
lines.en: You are partly right, but someone has to look at the whole country, not just one area.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Lako je tako govoriti iz velikoga grada. Naša županija godinama čeka novu bolnicu.
lines.en: Easy to talk like that from a big city. Our county has been waiting years for a new hospital.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Razumijem te, ali novac je jedan, a potrebe su svugdje. Pitanje je reda, a ne zle volje.
lines.en: I understand you, but there is one budget and needs are everywhere. It is a question of order, not ill will.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Onda neka vlast bar dođe i objasni taj red ljudima u lice.
lines.en: Then let the authorities at least come and explain that order to people's faces.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: E, s tim se potpuno slažem. Odluka bez objašnjenja izgleda kao nepravda i kad to nije.
lines.en: Now with that I fully agree. A decision without an explanation looks like injustice even when it isn't.

days.title: Novac danas: euro i kuna
days.objective: Talk about money, prices and saving in today's Croatia and defend a spending or saving habit with a reason.
days.paretoFocus: The deck teaches no current money vocabulary; this hosts euro, placa, racun, trosak, stednja, iznos and cijena, and frames kuna correctly as recent history rather than live pricing.
drills: Say aloud three regular monthly expenses you have, each with an approximate amount in euros.
Explain in two sentences one thing you save money on and one thing you never save on.
Turn a kuna price your grandparents might quote into today's euro reality in one sentence.

items: Which case do nouns take after the numbers pet, deset and higher?
How do you say 'according to my experience' with the preposition prema?
What is the standard Croatian word for a bill or invoice?

activities.title: Euro, plaća i štednja
activities.intro: Croatia switched to the euro in 2023, so euro is the everyday currency and kuna survives only as a memory. Watch plaća (salary), račun (bill), trošak (expense) and štednja (saving), all high-frequency in real budgets.

items.hr: Otkad smo prešli na euro, lakše uspoređujem cijene na putovanju, iako mislim da su neki trgovci cijene zaokružili naviše.
items.en: Since we switched to the euro, I compare prices more easily when travelling, though I think some shopkeepers rounded prices upward.
items.note: euro = the current currency

items.hr: Kuna je bila naša valuta do 2023., a danas je spominjemo samo kad se prisjećamo starih cijena.
items.en: The kuna was our currency until 2023, and today we mention it only when we recall old prices.
items.note: kuna = former currency, now history

items.hr: Svaki mjesec odvojim dio plaće za štednju jer smatram da čovjek bez zalihe živi u stalnom strahu.
items.en: Every month I set aside part of my salary for savings because I believe a person without a reserve lives in constant fear.
items.note: plaća = salary; štednja = saving

items.hr: Kad mi stigne račun za struju, prvo provjerim je li iznos veći nego prošli mjesec.
items.en: When my electricity bill arrives, the first thing I check is whether the amount is higher than last month.
items.note: račun = bill; iznos = amount

items.hr: Najveći nam je trošak najam stana, pa pokušavamo uštedjeti na svemu ostalome.
items.en: Our biggest expense is the flat rent, so we try to save on everything else.
items.note: trošak = expense; uštedjeti = to save (perfective)

activities.title: Cijene, računi i štednja
activities.intro: Practise the language of everyday money in modern Croatia.

questions.prompt: Danas u trgovini uvijek plaćam u ___.
questions.explanation: The euro is today's currency; 'u eurima' is the locative plural. Kunama would name the old currency, and euru and eurom are the wrong forms here.

questions.prompt: U novčaniku mi je na kraju mjeseca ostalo samo pet ___.
questions.explanation: A quick return to numbers: from five upward the noun stands in the genitive plural, so euro becomes eura.

questions.prompt: Ne kupujem na kredit; radije platim sve odmah u ___.
questions.explanation: Gotovina means cash; 'u gotovini' (in cash) puts it in the locative.

questions.prompt: Struja je opet poskupjela, pa mi je ovaj mjesec ___ za režije bio previsok.
questions.explanation: The thing that arrives and is too high is the račun (bill), the nominative subject of bio.

questions.prompt: Build the Croatian for: 'I want to save a little money every month.'
questions.explanation: Željeti takes the infinitive uštedjeti; malo is followed by the genitive novca, and svaki mjesec gives the time.

activities.title: Je li euro sve poskupio?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Your friend blames the euro for higher prices; you weigh what really changed and what only seems so.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Otkad imamo euro, sve je skuplje. Kava, kruh, najam, sve je poskupjelo.
lines.en: Since we have the euro, everything is more expensive. Coffee, bread, rent, everything went up.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Dio toga jest istina, ali nije kriv euro nego trgovci koji su cijene zaokružili naviše.
lines.en: Part of that is true, but the euro isn't to blame, the shopkeepers who rounded prices up are.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Svejedno, prije sam s istom plaćom mogao kupiti više.
lines.en: All the same, before I could buy more with the same salary.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Slažem se da plaće ne prate cijene, no to je bio problem i dok smo imali kunu.
lines.en: I agree that salaries don't keep up with prices, but that was a problem even while we had the kuna.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Barem sam tada odmah znao koliko nešto vrijedi, bez preračunavanja.
lines.en: At least then I knew right away how much something was worth, without any converting.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: To je prošlo za koji mjesec. Meni je danas lakše jer na putovanju odmah vidim je li nešto skupo.
lines.en: That passed within a couple of months. For me it's easier now because when travelling I immediately see whether something is expensive.

days.title: Sport i igra
days.objective: Talk about matches, teams and results and defend what you value most in sport, the win or the game itself.
days.paretoFocus: Hosts the spoken absentee igra plus the sport core the deck lacks: momcad, ekipa, utakmica, pobijediti, pobjeda, poraz, navijati and rezultat.
drills: Describe your favourite sport in three sentences, naming the team, a result and how you felt.
Say aloud one reason a loss can be more useful than a win.
Invite a friend to watch a match with you, giving the day and the opponent.

items: When does the imperfective past describe an action that was still going on?
How does an accusative feminine noun like utakmica end after a verb such as igrati?
What is the standard Croatian word for football?

activities.title: Utakmica, momčad i rezultat
activities.intro: Sport talk is everywhere in Croatia. Note igra (the game, and play), momčad and ekipa (team), pobijediti (to win) and its opposites pobjeda and poraz. Each sentence also takes a small position.

items.hr: Nogomet je kod nas više od igre; za mnoge je to način života.
items.en: Football here is more than a game; for many it is a way of life.
items.note: igra = game, play; nogomet = football

items.hr: Naša je momčad sinoć pobijedila u zadnjoj minuti, pa je pola grada slavilo do jutra.
items.en: Our team won last night in the final minute, so half the city celebrated until morning.
items.note: momčad = team; pobijediti = to win

items.hr: Volim sport u kojem se cijeni ekipa, a ne samo jedan igrač.
items.en: I like the kind of sport where the team is valued, not just a single player.
items.note: ekipa = team, crew; igrač = player

items.hr: Kad navijam, ne zanima me samo rezultat nego i lijepa igra.
items.en: When I cheer, I don't care only about the result but also about a good game.
items.note: navijati = to cheer, support; rezultat = result

items.hr: Poraz me nauči više nego pobjeda jer me natjera da promijenim nešto u treningu.
items.en: A defeat teaches me more than a win because it forces me to change something in my training.
items.note: poraz = defeat; pobjeda = victory

activities.title: Na utakmici
activities.intro: Practise talking about games, teams and results.

questions.prompt: Sutra igramo važnu ___ protiv prošlogodišnjeg prvaka.
questions.explanation: Igrati takes a direct object in the accusative; feminine utakmica becomes utakmicu.

questions.prompt: Dok smo ___ na treningu, iznenada je počela kiša.
questions.explanation: A look back at aspect: the imperfective igrali sets the ongoing background against which the sudden rain (perfective) happens.

questions.prompt: Navijači su bili tužni jer je njihova ___ izgubila u finalu.
questions.explanation: The team is the subject: momčad, a feminine i-noun that stays the same in the nominative singular.

questions.prompt: Nakon devedeset minuta konačni ___ bio je dva prema jedan.
questions.explanation: The final score is the rezultat, the nominative subject; note 'dva prema jedan' with the preposition prema.

questions.prompt: Build the Croatian for: 'Our team won the match.'
questions.explanation: The clitic je sits in second position; pobijediti is intransitive here, so the game appears as 'na utakmici' in the locative.

activities.title: Je li važna samo pobjeda?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Your friend cares only about winning; you argue that how a team plays matters too.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Za mene je bitna samo pobjeda. Lijepa igra bez rezultata ništa ne vrijedi.
lines.en: For me only the win matters. A beautiful game without a result is worth nothing.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ne slažem se. Radije gledam ekipu koja igra hrabro i izgubi nego onu koja se brani i pobijedi.
